coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized gold nanoparticles for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging zero dimensional materials promising various novel applications due to their extraordinary physicochemical properties. In this work, novel conjugates of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are synthesized and used for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions (Hg2+ and Cu2+). Ultralow detection limit (0.02nM with S/N=6.25 for Hg2+ and 0.05nM with S/N=4.81 for Cu2+) and high sensitivity (2.47μA/nM for Hg2+ and 3.69μA/nM for Cu2+) have been achieved.

Gold nanoparticles
Heavy metal detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra. (B) Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) of AuNPs drop-casted on glassy carbon electrode (GCE). (C) SEM of GQD-AuNPs on GCE. (D) Energy dispersive spectroscopy.
FTIR spectra of GQD (A) and GQD-AuNP (B).

(A) Anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) of GQD-AuNP modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) in the presence of 0.1μM of Hg2+. (B) ASV curves of GQD-AuNP electrode with various concentrations of Hg2+. (C) Average dose-response curve from 3 different electrodes (error bars indicate standard deviation). The fitted line has a slope of 0.467μA/nM. (D) Expanded view of the boxed region in C. The fitted line at lower concentration range has a slope of 2.47μA/nM.

(A) Anodic stripping voltammetry curves with different concentrations of Cu2+. (B) Average dose-response curve from 3 different electrodes (error bars indicate standard deviation). The fitting is linear in logarithmic scale. (C) Comparison of anodic stripping with 0.5μM Hg2+, 0.5μM Cu2+ and a mixture of both.
Electrode stability in response to 0.5nM Hg2+ and 0.5nM Cu2+ over 7 days.
Determination of Hg2+ or Cu2+ concentration based on calibrated response curves.
Comparisons of performances between various heavy metal ion sensors.

row.entry: Heated graphite nanoparticle screen printed electrode (SPE)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized gold nanoparticles for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 70 Nanyang Drive, Singapore 637457, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: GlobalFoundries Singapore, 60 Woodlands Industrial Park D Street 2, Singapore 738406, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging zero dimensional materials promising various novel applications due to their extraordinary physicochemical properties. In this work, novel conjugates of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are synthesized and used for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions (Hg2+ and Cu2+). Ultralow detection limit (0.02nM with S/N=6.25 for Hg2+ and 0.05nM with S/N=4.81 for Cu2+) and high sensitivity (2.47μA/nM for Hg2+ and 3.69μA/nM for Cu2+) have been achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: Contamination of heavy metal ions (e.g., mercury) is of serious concern to human health. Accumulation of mercury ions (Hg2+) poses many dangerous issues such as renal and neural problems [1,2]. According to US Environment Protection Agency, the safety limit of Hg2+ in water is as low as 10nM to avoid potential health problems [3]. It is of a great challenge to detect such trace amount. Electrochemical sensing provides possibilities for rapid, sensitive, and on-site detection of heavy metal ions [4].

Graphene, a two-dimensional single-atom-thin carbon crystalline sheet, has been widely used for electrochemical sensor development owing to its high electrical conductivity, large surface area, wide electrochemical potential window, chemical stability, biocompatibility, and highly tunable properties by chemical modification or heteroatom doping [5,6]. Recently, graphene quantum dots are emerging as a new class of zero dimensional materials [7,8], promising novel applications in bio-imaging [9], optical sensing [10]. Optical detection of Hg2+ [11] and Fe3+ [12] has been demonstrated using GQDs. For electrochemical sensing, GQD modified graphite electrode has been used for sensitive detection of ssDNA molecules [13]. GQDs have also been covalently assembled on gold electrode for electrochemical sensing of hydrogen peroxide [14].

Gold nanoparticles (AuNP) have been widely employed for sensor development including electrochemical sensors [15]. For examples, AuNP decorated carbon nanotubes [16] and AuNPs conjugated with thymine containing oligonucleotides [16] have been used to electrochemically detect the low presence Hg2+ ions. It has also been demonstrated that hybridizing AuNPs with graphene oxide sheets can enhance the performance of electrochemical detection [17]. In this work, GQDs are functionalized onto cysteamine-capped gold nanoparticles for electrochemical detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.02nM, using anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) method. The sensor can also detect Cu2+ at a concentration as low as 0.05nM.

All chemicals are purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Electrochemical measurements were carried out with CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments Inc.) using the three-electrode system consisting of a platinum counter electrode, standard calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference, and GQD-AuNP modified GCE as the working electrode (0.071cm2). The electrode surface morphology and X-ray energy dispersive spectrometry (XRD) of the sample were examined using field emission scanning microscope (JEOL JSM-6700F). UV–vis and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were obtained from a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer and a PerkinElmer Spectrum GX FTIR system, respectively.

GQDs were synthesized as previously described [18]. Briefly, CX-72 carbon black was refluxed in concentrated nitric acid for 24h, followed by centrifugation (7000rpm). The supernatant was then fully dried and re-dissolved in deionized water to defined concentrations. Cysteamine-capped AuNPs were synthesized via chemical reduction of gold chloride solution [19]. In brief, 40mL of HAuCl4 (1.42mM) and 400μL of of cysteamine (213mM) were mixed and stirred for 20min in dark. 10μL of NaBH3 was added with continuous stirring in dark for 10min. The obtained cysteamine-AuNP solution (5mL) was then mixed with GQD solution (5mL at 2mg/mL), followed by addition of 40mg ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) with shaking for 1h. The solution was then centrifuged at 10krpm for 10min to obtain the sediment. This process is repeated for 5 times to thoroughly remove the excess GQDs. Finally the sediment was re-dispersed into 1mL of deionized water.

The glassy carbon electrode was first polished using alumina powder, followed by thorough washing (with ethanol and deionized water) and sonication. After blow-drying using nitrogen gas, 3μL of prepared GQD-AuNPs solution was drop-casted onto the electrode surface and left to dry 30min. The electrode was further dried in vacuum for another 10min. Finally, 3μL of 5% nafion solution was drop-coated onto the modified electrode as the proton exchange membrane and protection layer.

Preconcentration of the electrode was performed by immersing the electrode in the electrolyte (0.1M HCl) containing the target analyte (Hg2+ or Cu2+) at a defined concentration, with −0.2V applied for 120s. Anodic stripping was carried out using square wave voltammetry at frequency of 40Hz, amplitude of 20mV and potential increment step of 4mV.

GQDs (∼2nm in lateral dimension, single to a few layered) synthesized from carbon black by acidic exfoliation [9,18] bear abundant negatively charged carboxyl groups, making its high dispersible in solutions (yellowish color). Cysteamine-capped AuNPs (dark red) possess amine groups as the chemical handles for covalent bonding with carboxylated GQDs, producing a purple colored dispersion. In addition to the color change, the success of functionalization is also evidenced by the UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 1 A). In consistent with the previous reports [12], GQDs efficiently absorb UV light. The absorption peak of AuNPs at 533nm indicates a particle size of ∼50nm. This is in agreement with observation by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 1B). In comparison, the absorption peak of GQD-AuNPs is red-shifted and broadened, suggesting clustering of particles (likely resulting from neutralization of the positive charges on cysteamine-AuNP by attachment of negatively-charged GQDs). As revealed by SEM, the individual particle size however is not significantly increased because of the small size of GQDs (Fig. 1C).

The spectrum of energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) exhibits characteristic peaks of carbon, oxygen and Au, further confirming the success of conjugation (Fig. 1D). Moreover, the conjugation is also evidenced by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The FTIR spectrum of GQD-AuNPs demonstrates the characteristic peaks of N-H and C-N bonding (resulting from the reaction between carboxyl and amine groups) as well as peaks of O-H and C-O (from the unreacted oxygenated groups on GQDs) (Fig. 2 ).

Anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) of GQD-AuNP modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) in the presence of 0.1μM of Hg2+ is shown in Fig. 3 A. A peak at 0.42V is observed because of electrochemical oxidation of Hg. The stripping voltammetry of the AuNP-coated GCE electrode only shows a weak oxidative peak, while GQD-coated GCE electrode do not give obvious response (just like the bare GCE electrode) probably because it is difficult to immobilize the highly soluble GQDs onto the electrode surface even with a Nafion protection layer. As schematically shown in Fig. 4 , Hg2+ ions are pre-concentrated onto the electrode by an initially applied negative voltage (−0.2V for 120s) and the negatively charged carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs [20]. It has been proposed that carboxyl groups interact with Hg2+ in the form of R-COO-(Hg2+)-OOC-R [21]. In addition, AuNPs also have high affinity towards mercury [22]. Under square wave voltammetry, the absorbed mercury is subsequently oxidized (anodic stripping). As compared with GQD-AuNP modified GCE, AuNP modified electrode gives a much weaker oxidation peak while oxidation is absent for bare GCE. Clearly, the synergistic integration between GQDs and AuNPs is important. As shown in Fig. 3B-D, the response of GQD-AuNP electrode is dose-dependent with a detection limit ∼ 0.02nM (S/N=6.25). In the low concentration range (0.02 to 1.5nM), linear response with a high sensitivity of 2.47μA/nM is achieved. Beyond 1.5nM, the sensitivity is reduced to 0.467μA/nM and the linear response remains up to 100nM. These observations suggest that the dominating binding mechanism differ at low and high concentration regions.

The sensor is also applied for detection of Cu2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the anodic stripping potential of copper is at ∼0V which is clearly separated from that of mercury, suggesting the possibility of simultaneous detection of the two ion species. The detection limit for Cu2+ ions is 0.05nM (S/N=4.81). However, the current response scales logarithmically (instead of linearly) with the increasing concentration (Fig. 5B). Fig. 5C shows the stripping voltammetry in the presence of both Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. It is observed that the current peaks from the two types of ions remain well-separated. However, it is noted that the Hg2+ peak is largely reduced. It is conceivable that the two types of ions compete for the limited binding sites on the electrode surface, and Cu2+ has higher binding affinity. Other metals such as Pb2+ and Fe2+ are also tested. However, no obvious response is observed for Pb2+ (stripping potential at −0.5V) [23] and Fe2+ (−0.7V) [24] partly because bubbling is resulted when negative potential is applied on the electrode.

Fig. 6 shows the stability of the prepared electrode over a week towards mercury and copper ions detection. The response remains fairly stable within the first week of the electrode fabrication. Experiments are also conducted to access the ion concentration of a spiked electrolyte solution based on the calibrated response curves (Fig. 3 and 5). As shown in Table 1 , satisfactory accuracy can be attained. Comparisons to various electrochemical detection of heavy metal using anodic stripping voltammetry methods are summarized in Table 2 , showing the superior performance of our GQD-AuNP electrode.

In conclusion, a highly sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor for heavy metal ions (Hg2+ and Cu2+) is developed. The high performance can be attributed to the synergist cooperation between GQDs and AuNPs. It promises applications in environmental monitoring and safety assessment. This study also demonstrates the potential of the emerging GQDs in electrochemical sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is supported by AcRF Tier 2 grants from Ministry of Education of Singapore (MOE2011-T2-2-010, MOE2014-T2-1-003). We also thank GlobalFoundries (Singapore) for the scholarship provided to S.L. Ting.
